section.title: The Paralytic, Levi, and the Sabbath
section.sectionRationale: Mark gathers a first cluster of controversies. Jesus forgives and heals a paralytic, claiming a right belonging to God alone; He calls a tax collector and eats with sinners; He answers questions about fasting with the joy of the bridegroom; and He declares Himself Lord of the Sabbath. Opposition begins to gather even as the good news widens to the outsider.
section.thesis: The Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins and to reinterpret the Sabbath, and He came not to call the righteous but sinners, so that His arrival is like a wedding feast and a new wine that the old forms cannot contain.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the paralytic forgiven and healed; the call of Levi and the meal with sinners; the question about fasting and the bridegroom; the parables of patch and wineskins; and the Lord of the Sabbath.

units.label: The paralytic forgiven and healed
units.note: the Son of Man has authority to forgive

verses.text: A few days later Jesus went back to Capernaum. And when the people heard that He was home,
they gathered in such large numbers that there was no more room, not even outside the door, as Jesus spoke the word to them.
Then a paralytic was brought to Him, carried by four men.
Since they were unable to get to Jesus through the crowd, they uncovered the roof above Him, made an opening, and lowered the paralytic on his mat.
When Jesus saw their faith, He said to the paralytic, “Son, your sins are {{aphiemi|forgiven}}.”
But some of the scribes were sitting there and thinking in their hearts,
“Why does this man speak like this? He is blaspheming! Who can forgive sins but God alone?”
At once Jesus knew in His spirit that they were thinking this way within themselves. “Why are you thinking these things in your hearts?” He asked.
“Which is easier: to say to a paralytic, ‘Your sins are forgiven,’ or to say, ‘Get up, pick up your mat, and walk’?
But so that you may know that the {{huiosanthropou|Son of Man}} has authority on earth to forgive sins...” He said to the paralytic,
“I tell you, get up, pick up your mat, and go home.”
And immediately the man got up, picked up his mat, and walked out in front of them all. As a result, they were all astounded and glorified God, saying, “We have never seen anything like this!”

units.label: The call of Levi and the meal with sinners
units.note: I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners

verses.text: Once again Jesus went out beside the sea. All the people came to Him, and He taught them there.
As He was walking along, He saw Levi son of Alphaeus sitting at the tax booth. “Follow Me,” He told him, and Levi got up and followed Him.
While Jesus was dining at Levi’s house, many tax collectors and {{hamartolos|sinners}} were eating with Him and His disciples, for there were many who followed Him.
When the scribes who were Pharisees saw Jesus eating with these people, they asked His disciples, “Why does He eat with tax collectors and sinners?”
On hearing this, Jesus told them, “It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick. I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners.”

units.label: The question about fasting and the bridegroom
units.note: how can the guests fast while the bridegroom is here

verses.text: Now John’s disciples and the Pharisees were often fasting. So people came to Jesus and asked, “Why don’t Your disciples fast like John’s disciples and those of the Pharisees?”
Jesus replied, “How can the guests of the {{nymphios|bridegroom}} fast while He is with them? As long as He is with them, they cannot fast.
But the time will come when the bridegroom will be taken from them; then they will fast.
No one sews a patch of unshrunk cloth on an old garment. If he does, the new piece will pull away from the old, and a worse tear will result.
And no one pours new wine into old wineskins. If he does, the wine will burst the skins, and both the wine and the wineskins will be ruined. Instead, new wine is poured into new wineskins.”

units.label: The Lord of the Sabbath
units.note: the Sabbath was made for man

verses.text: One Sabbath Jesus was passing through the grainfields, and His disciples began to pick the heads of grain as they walked along.
So the Pharisees said to Him, “Look, why are they doing what is unlawful on the Sabbath?”
Jesus replied, “Have you never read what David did when he and his companions were hungry and in need?
During the high priesthood of Abiathar, he entered the house of God and ate the consecrated bread, which was lawful only for the priests. And he gave some to his companions as well.”
Then Jesus declared, “The {{sabbaton|Sabbath}} was made for man, not man for the Sabbath.
Therefore, the Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath.”

aphiemi.gloss: To send away, release, forgive. To forgive sins is to remit the debt owed to God, cancelling guilt. The scribes rightly know this is God's prerogative, which is why Jesus' words provoke the charge of blasphemy, and why His healing proves His right to speak them.

huiosanthropou.gloss: Son of Man, Jesus' favourite self-designation in Mark. It draws on Daniel 7:13-14, where one like a son of man is given everlasting dominion by God. The title holds together true humanity and divine authority, here the authority on earth to forgive sins.

hamartolos.gloss: A sinner, one outside the bounds of the law-keeping community, morally and socially disreputable. Jesus deliberately shares table with such people, declaring that He came precisely to call sinners, not those who reckon themselves righteous.

nymphios.gloss: Bridegroom. In the Old Testament the LORD is the husband of His people; Jesus takes the title for Himself, so His presence is a wedding celebration. The hint that the bridegroom will be taken away is the first quiet foreshadowing of the cross.

sabbaton.gloss: The Sabbath, the seventh-day rest commanded in the law as a gift to humanity. Jesus insists the Sabbath was made to serve people, not to enslave them, and claims to be its Lord, the one with authority to say what the day truly means.

basic.context: Mark lines up five scenes where Jesus collides with the religious leaders. Four friends dig through a roof to lower a paralyzed man to Jesus, who forgives his sins, then heals him to prove He has the right to forgive. He calls Levi, a despised tax collector, and shares a meal with tax collectors and sinners, saying He came for the sick, not the self-satisfied. Asked why His disciples do not fast, He says you cannot fast at a wedding while the bridegroom is present. His newness cannot be patched onto the old system. And He declares Himself Lord of the Sabbath.
basic.hermeneutics: Read these as a series building one case: Jesus claims prerogatives that belong to God. He forgives sins, welcomes sinners, calls Himself the bridegroom of God's people, and reinterprets the Sabbath as its Lord. The opposition that begins here will grow until it turns deadly. The healing is offered as visible proof of an invisible reality, forgiveness.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus tells the paralytic his sins are aphiemi, forgiven, a word for the release of a debt only God can grant. He calls Himself the huiosanthropou, the Son of Man of Daniel 7, with authority on earth to forgive. He came to call hamartolos, sinners. He is the nymphios, the bridegroom, whose presence is joy. And He is Lord of the sabbaton, the Sabbath.
basic.typology: David eating the consecrated bread foreshadows the greater Son of David who has authority over the holy things. The bridegroom imagery, drawn from the prophets who called the LORD Israel's husband, points to Jesus as the divine bridegroom. The new wine and new cloth signal the new covenant breaking in.
basic.christ: Jesus does what only God can do: He forgives sins. He calls Himself the Son of Man with divine authority, the bridegroom of God's people, and the Lord of the Sabbath. His words and works together announce that God Himself has come to save sinners.
basic.god: Only God can forgive sins, the scribes say, and they are right, which is exactly the point. In Jesus, God draws near to forgive, to welcome sinners to His table, and to give His people rest. God's holiness and His mercy meet in the Son of Man.
basic.crossReferences: Daniel 7 gives the Son of Man his dominion. 1 Samuel 21 tells of David and the consecrated bread. Isaiah 62 and Hosea 2 present the LORD as bridegroom, fulfilled in Christ.
basic.oneStory: God had promised to forgive His people's sins, to feast with them, and to give them rest. In Jesus all of this arrives. He forgives, He gathers sinners to the wedding table, and He is Lord of the Sabbath rest. The old forms cannot contain the new thing God is doing; the kingdom has come in the Son of Man.

context.literary: Mark 2:1 to 3:6 is a tight collection of five conflict stories, arranged so that opposition mounts steadily from private grumbling to a plot to kill Jesus. This study covers the first four and a half; the fifth, the withered hand, opens the next study. The scenes are joined by rising tension and by Jesus' repeated claims to unique authority.
context.historical: Roofs in Galilean homes were flat, of beams, branches, and packed earth, so digging through one was feasible though dramatic. Tax collectors gathered tolls for Rome and were despised as collaborators and cheats. Table fellowship implied acceptance, so eating with sinners was scandalous to the Pharisees, who fasted regularly beyond the one required annual fast.
context.canonical: The Son of Man title reaches back to Daniel 7:13-14 and becomes Jesus' chief self-title. The bridegroom imagery draws on the prophets and reappears in the wedding of the Lamb, Revelation 19. The new wine and garment picture the new covenant that Jeremiah 31 promised and Hebrews expounds.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Jesus claims and exercises authority that belongs to God alone, forgiving sins, welcoming sinners, and mastering the Sabbath, and to trace the rise of opposition that this provokes, so the reader sees both who Jesus is and why He is resisted.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The healing of the paralytic is a sign, not a promise that faith always brings physical healing. What is prescriptive is the reality of forgiveness in Christ, His welcome of sinners, and the truth that the Sabbath is God's gift for human good. The specific customs of first century fasting are described, not commanded.

debates.question: In what sense does the Son of Man have authority to forgive sins?

views.label: A divine prerogative exercised by Jesus
views.case: The scribes are right that only God forgives; Jesus' claim, backed by the healing, is an implicit claim to divine authority, that in Him God Himself forgives.

views.label: Authority delegated to the Danielic Son of Man
views.case: Daniel 7 gives the Son of Man dominion from the Ancient of Days; Jesus exercises a God-given authority on earth, which the miracle publicly validates.

debates.bearing: Either way the healing proves the forgiveness is real and Jesus' authority genuine; the deepest need met at the roof-opening is not paralysis but sin.

debates.question: Does Jesus' teaching abolish the Sabbath or fulfil it?

views.label: He restores its true purpose
views.case: The Sabbath was made for man's good; Jesus rejects the burdensome traditions that had buried it, recovering it as a gift, not a cage.

views.label: He claims lordship that points to a greater rest
views.case: As Lord of the Sabbath, Jesus is the one to whom the day pointed; the rest it signalled is finally found in Him, Hebrews 4:9-10, so its observance is transformed under the new covenant.

debates.bearing: Both agree Jesus is Lord over the Sabbath, not its servant; the day's purpose, rest and mercy for people, is fulfilled and mastered in Him.

study.originalLanguages: The pivot of the first scene is aphiemi, to forgive, the release of a debt that the scribes rightly say only God can grant. Jesus answers by naming Himself the huiosanthropou, the Son of Man of Daniel 7, who has authority on earth to forgive, and He proves it by a word that raises the paralytic. He then declares He came to call hamartolos, sinners, sharing their table. Asked about fasting, He calls Himself the nymphios, the bridegroom, whose presence turns fasting to feasting until the day He is taken away. Finally He claims to be Lord of the sabbaton, the Sabbath God made for human good.

typology.fulfillment: The greater Son of David, Jesus, who has authority over the holy things and provides for His hungry people, 1 Samuel 21:1-6 and Matthew 12:3-4.
Christ the divine bridegroom, foreshadowed by the LORD as Israel's husband and consummated at the wedding of the Lamb, Hosea 2:19 and Revelation 19:7.
The rest of the people of God found in Christ, Lord of the Sabbath, Hebrews 4:9-10.

study.christ: In these scenes Jesus does what belongs to God alone. He forgives the paralytic's sins, a divine prerogative, and proves His right by healing him with a word. He calls Himself the Son of Man of Daniel 7, invested with authority on earth. He welcomes the very sinners the righteous despised, declaring that He came for them. He names Himself the bridegroom, taking a title the prophets reserved for the LORD, and hints that He will be taken away, the first shadow of the cross. And He claims to be Lord of the Sabbath. Every scene lifts the curtain a little further on the identity Mark announced in his first verse.
study.god: The scribes speak better than they know: who can forgive sins but God alone? The whole passage answers that in Jesus, God has drawn near to do exactly that. God forgives the guilty, welcomes sinners to His table, celebrates like a bridegroom with His people, and gives the Sabbath as a gift for human flourishing. The holiness that made the scribes recoil and the mercy that reached the outcast are both God's, and both meet in the Son of Man.

crossReferences.note: One like a son of man given everlasting dominion and authority, the background to Jesus' favourite self-title.
David eating the consecrated bread, cited by Jesus to answer the Sabbath charge.
The LORD betrothing His people to Himself, background to Jesus as the bridegroom.
The Sabbath rest that remains for God's people, fulfilled in Christ, Lord of the Sabbath.

study.oneStory: God had promised to blot out His people's sins, to feast with them as a husband with his bride, and to give them true rest. In Jesus these promises arrive at once. He forgives sins with a word, gathers tax collectors and sinners to the wedding table, and declares Himself Lord of the Sabbath. The new wine of the kingdom cannot be poured into the old forms; something greater has come. Yet already the bridegroom speaks of being taken away, and the opposition gathering here will carry the story toward the cross where sins are actually paid for.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus first say to the paralytic, your sins are forgiven, before healing him?

options.text: To meet the man's deepest need and to display His divine authority to forgive, which the healing then proves.
options.note: Jesus makes forgiveness the point and heals to show the Son of Man's authority to forgive, Mark 2:10.

options.text: Because forgiveness was easier to say than to heal.
options.note: Jesus does note it is easy to say, but His aim was to prove that the unseen forgiveness was real, not merely easy words.

options.text: Because the man had asked to be forgiven rather than healed.
options.note: The text does not record such a request; Jesus took the initiative to address sin first.

options.text: Because sin and paralysis are always directly linked.
options.note: Jesus does not teach that this man's paralysis was caused by a specific sin; He addresses the universal need of forgiveness.

quiz.q: Why did the scribes accuse Jesus of blasphemy?

options.text: Because He claimed to forgive sins, which they knew only God can do.
options.note: Mark 2:7, who can forgive sins but God alone; they rightly saw a divine claim.

options.text: Because He healed on the wrong day.
options.note: That charge arises elsewhere; here the offence was specifically His claim to forgive sins.

options.text: Because He touched an unclean person.
options.note: That was an issue with the leper, not the paralytic; the blasphemy charge concerned forgiving sins.

options.text: Because He refused to heal the man.
options.note: Jesus did heal him; the offence was His claim to forgive.

quiz.q: What did Jesus mean by saying He came to call not the righteous but sinners?

options.text: He came to save those who know they are sinners in need of mercy, like the sick who need a doctor.
options.note: Mark 2:17 gives the doctor and the sick as His own explanation.

options.text: He preferred the company of disreputable people to respectable ones.
options.note: He did eat with sinners, but the point is their need of a Saviour, not a mere preference for their company.

options.text: There are some genuinely righteous people who do not need Him.
options.note: The self-styled righteous also need Him; the irony is that they will not admit their sickness.

options.text: He came only for the outwardly moral and religious.
options.note: The opposite: He came for sinners, not the self-satisfied righteous.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus say His disciples do not fast now?

options.text: Because the bridegroom is present, and guests cannot fast at a wedding; they will fast when He is taken away.
options.note: Mark 2:19-20 gives the bridegroom image and the hint of His being taken away.

options.text: Because fasting is no longer a valid practice for anyone.
options.note: Jesus says they will fast when the bridegroom is taken; He does not abolish fasting outright.

options.text: Because His disciples were not spiritual enough to fast.
options.note: It was the joy of the bridegroom's presence, not a lack of devotion, that made fasting unfitting then.

options.text: Because fasting was invented by the Pharisees.
options.note: Fasting was a genuine biblical practice; Jesus addresses its fitting time, not its origin.

quiz.q: What did Jesus claim about the Sabbath?

options.text: The Sabbath was made for man's good, and the Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath.
options.note: Mark 2:27-28 gives both truths together.

options.text: The Sabbath was made for man's good and should be kept flexibly.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but it omits Jesus' central claim to be Lord of the Sabbath.

options.text: The Sabbath no longer matters at all.
options.note: Jesus honours the Sabbath as God's gift; He claims lordship over it rather than dismissing it.

options.text: People exist to serve the Sabbath's rules.
options.note: Jesus says the reverse: the Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath.

questions: Four friends went to great lengths to bring their paralyzed friend to Jesus. Who in your life needs you to carry them, persistently, to Christ?
Jesus addressed the man's deepest need, forgiveness, before his obvious one. What is the deeper need beneath the things you most often ask God to fix?
Jesus ate with tax collectors and sinners and was criticized for it. How does His welcome of sinners shape whom you are willing to draw near to?
The self-styled righteous missed their need of a doctor. Where might you be trusting your own respectability rather than owning your need of mercy?
Jesus is Lord even of the Sabbath and offers true rest. How could you receive the Sabbath as a gift for your good rather than a burden or an afterthought?

translationNotes.note: The sentence breaks off mid-thought in the Greek as Jesus turns from the scribes to the paralytic; the original used a dash, rendered here with three dots to show the shift of address.

During the high priesthood of Abiathar has occasioned much discussion, since 1 Samuel 21 names Ahimelech; the phrase likely means in the days of Abiathar, a well-known figure of that priestly period, or in the section of Scripture associated with him.
Some manuscripts omit the closing line, new wine is poured into new wineskins; the well-attested reading includes it, sealing the point that the new age needs new forms.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 2:1-28. Footnote markers and line breaks were dropped when assembling verse text. The em dash in verse 15 was converted to a comma; verse 10's dash rendered as an ellipsis to show the break in address. No em or en dashes remain in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
